one_line_takeaway: Before trusting LLMs in smart-grid pipelines, note that off-the-shelf models -- GPT-3.5/4, Gemini 2.0, LLaMA-3, DeepSeek-V3 -- can be trivially prompt-injected into misclassifying grid incident reports and into fully dumping protected domain data despite explicit refusal instructions.
key_finding: All five LLMs are highly vulnerable to both attacks. On the clean classification task the LLMs match or beat the prior specialized classifier with only prompt engineering, but crafted injection text collapses their performance across all three attack variants. For domain knowledge extraction, all five models refused the naive query in 100/100 tests yet leaked the full protected data in 100/100 tests when faced with the crafted Python-dictionary extraction prompt -- a complete (100%) failure of the confidentiality guardrail under a mildly sophisticated query.
core_contribution: The paper systematically scopes and threat-models the security risks specific to deploying pre-trained LLMs in smart-grid applications, then empirically validates them. Its own contributions are: (1) a scoping argument distinguishing LLM-specific smart-grid risks from generic LLM issues (jailbreaks, provider-side privacy, unauthorized public-LLM access are explicitly excluded from scope); (2) two generalized threat models -- 'bad data injection' by an external adversary who can modify legitimate inputs but cannot see LLM output, and 'domain knowledge extraction' by internal/external actors who can query and read output; and (3) proof-of-concept validation of both attacks across five popular LLMs using real smart-grid data. Note that the ML/NLP-in-power-grids and adversarial-attacks-on-smart-grid-ML work (Chen et al., Wang & Li, etc.) is Related Work the authors cite, not their contribution; the authors reuse Wang & Li's classification task as a target application but the threat modeling and attack validation are their own.
threat_model: Two distinct models. (1) Bad Data Injection: LLM ingests input from a public access point (e.g., processing public news/user feedback to detect grid incidents). Attacker can modify legitimate input via that public interface (e.g., man-in-the-middle) but CANNOT observe the LLM's output; attacker knows only the high-level purpose of the LLM. Goal is to force wrong outputs (misclassification) to mislead grid operators. (2) Domain Knowledge Extraction: LLM is prompted with sensitive raw grid data (system prompt / domain knowledge) but is instructed to return only aggregated summaries. Attacker has legitimate interactive access (can send inputs and read outputs) and high-level knowledge of the LLM's function; typically an insider, but extendable to outsiders if a user-facing chatbot is misconfigured. Goal is to coax the LLM into revealing the protected raw prompt data.
limitations: Proof-of-concept only: no defenses are proposed or evaluated (mitigation is left to future work). Attack payloads are trivial single-line prompt-injection strings rather than an adversarial optimization method, so the work shows susceptibility but not attack sophistication or robustness. Small evaluation scale -- 423 curated messages for injection and 100 tests per phase for extraction. Deployments are simulated in isolation, not in a real integrated smart-grid pipeline, and the bad-data-injection scenario assumes the attacker can perform MITM-style input modification. Results are tied to specific 2024-2025 model versions (incl. an 8B LLaMA variant) and a single hand-drafted system prompt per task, so guardrail strength may differ with better prompting or newer models. The domain-knowledge data (EIA-861M public form) is not actually confidential; it stands in for sensitive grid configuration data.

datasets_used: Wang & Li renewable-energy news incident dataset (reverted from tokens and manually curated to 423 labeled messages; binary Yes/No 'incident related' labels)
U.S. EIA-861M advanced metering infrastructure (AMI) monthly data, 6 selected columns: Month, Utility_Number, Name, State, AMR_Total, AMI_Total
quantitative_results: Normal-input classification accuracy: GPT-3.5 89.1%, GPT-4 93.8%, LLaMA-3 88.9%, Gemini 2.0 90.1%, DeepSeek-V3 89.8% (baseline REI classifier 88.5%)
Normal-input F1: GPT-3.5 88.7%, GPT-4 93.5%, LLaMA-3 88.8%, Gemini 2.0 88.9%, DeepSeek-V3 89.1% (baseline REI 91.4%)
Under false-positive injection, recall forced to 100% for all five models while accuracy dropped to ~47.7-59.3% (F1 ~64.6-70.1%)
Under false-negative injection, precision/recall/F1 driven to 0% for all five models with accuracy 52.2% across the board
Under reverse injection, accuracy collapsed to 33.5% (GPT-3.5), 43.4% (GPT-4), 33.8% (LLaMA-3), 34.0% (Gemini 2.0), 35.0% (DeepSeek-V3)

Domain knowledge extraction: all 5 models refused the naive dump query in 100/100 tests; all 5 models disclosed the full protected data in 100/100 tests under the crafted Python-dictionary extraction query
baselines_compared: REI Classifier from Wang & Li [24] (prior pre-trained-LM incident classifier): 88.5% accuracy, 89.0% precision, 93.8% recall, 91.4% F1
LLMs' own clean-input performance (Phase 1) serves as the internal baseline against which the injection-attack degradation (Phase 2) is measured
